mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Saudi Arabia on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia, on the Arabian Peninsula. Saudi Arabia takes up about 80% of the peninsula, with the Red Sea on one side and the Persian Gulf on the other 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Saudi Arabia?
mapQuestions.answer: Riyadh, in the centre of the country. About 7.5 million people. A fast-growing modern city in the middle of the desert, with the iconic Kingdom Centre tower and the new Diriyah heritage district.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Saudi Arabia speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Arabic. English is widely spoken in business, tourism and signage, especially in big cities.

mapQuestions.question: When did Saudi Arabia open to tourists?
mapQuestions.answer: In September 2019, Saudi Arabia introduced a tourist visa for the first time in its modern history. Before that, almost the only visitors to the country were on religious pilgrimage. Tourism is now growing fast.

mapQuestions.question: How important is religion in Saudi Arabia?
mapQuestions.answer: Very. Saudi Arabia is the historical centre of Islam. Mecca and Medina, the two holiest cities in Islam, are both here. Every year millions of Muslims travel to Mecca for the Hajj pilgrimage. Mecca and the central religious areas of Medina are restricted to Muslims only.

foods.name: Kabsa
foods.description: Saudi Arabia's national dish: long-grain basmati rice cooked with chicken or lamb, tomato, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves and saffron, topped with toasted nuts. Eaten communally from a big platter with the right hand.

foods.name: Mandi
foods.description: A Yemeni-Saudi tradition: meat (lamb, chicken or goat) and rice cooked together in a clay pit oven, the meat hung from hooks above the rice so the juices drip down. Smoky, tender, brilliant.

foods.name: Tamis
foods.description: Soft flatbread cooked on the inside wall of a tannour clay oven, served warm with every meal. Brought to Saudi tables by Afghan bakers and now beloved across the country.

foods.name: Luqaimat
foods.description: Crispy deep-fried dough balls drizzled in date or sugar syrup, dusted with sesame seeds. The Saudi tea-time sweet, especially during Ramadan.

foods.name: Saudi qahwa
foods.description: Saudi coffee: very light golden coffee flavoured with cardamom and sometimes saffron and rose water, served in small handle-less cups with fresh dates on the side. Hospitality in a cup.

foods.name: Dates
foods.description: Saudi Arabia grows hundreds of date varieties (Ajwa from Medina is the most famous and most expensive). Eaten with coffee, packed for travel, given as a gift. The desert's perfect food.

scavengerItems.label: The carved sandstone tombs of Hegra (Mada'in Salih) in AlUla
Elephant Rock, a natural sandstone arch shaped like an elephant in AlUla
The Empty Quarter (Rub' al Khali), the biggest continuous sand desert on Earth
A camel caravan or a single Bedouin riding through the dunes
Riyadh's Kingdom Centre tower with the famous "bottle opener" sky bridge
Sunset at the Edge of the World (Jebel Fihrayn) cliff face near Riyadh
A traditional brass coffee pot ("dallah") pouring qahwa into tiny cups
Red Sea coral reefs off Jeddah (some of the healthiest reefs in the world)
The historic Al-Balad district of Jeddah, with carved wooden balconies and coral-block walls (UNESCO)
An Arabian horse (the breed originated in Arabia and has been bred for 4,500 years)
A falconer with a hooded peregrine on his arm (falconry is a UNESCO heritage tradition here)

geoMatches.description: The capital. A fast-growing modern city in the heart of the desert. The Kingdom Centre tower, the new Diriyah historic mud-brick district, and the National Museum of Saudi Arabia.
A spectacular desert valley in the north-west, with sandstone cliffs, Elephant Rock, and the ancient Nabataean tombs of Hegra (Mada'in Salih). The country's biggest cultural tourism project.
Saudi Arabia's FIRST UNESCO World Heritage site (2008). 111 monumental rock-cut tombs from the Nabataean civilisation (the same people who built Petra in Jordan), dating to roughly 2,000 years ago.
The Red Sea coast city. Historically the port for pilgrims travelling to Mecca. The Al-Balad old town (UNESCO) has 500-year-old coral-stone tower houses with carved wooden balconies.
A 300-metre cliff face (Jebel Fihrayn) on the edge of an ancient seabed, about 90 minutes from Riyadh. The view at sunset feels like standing at the edge of the planet.
The cool, green south-western mountains, with stone-built villages, terraced fields, and the famous painted "qatt" walls of Rijal Almaa.

senses.smell: Cardamom in Saudi coffee, frankincense burning in a souq, hot desert sand at midday, fresh tamis bread baking in a tannour oven, ripe Ajwa dates from Medina…
senses.hear: The call to prayer drifting across Riyadh five times a day, "Marhaba!" greetings, Arabic radio in taxis, the silence of the Empty Quarter at night, the soft pour of Saudi coffee from a brass dallah…
senses.taste: Smoky kabsa rice with cinnamon and cardamom, soft warm tamis bread torn by hand, sticky syrupy luqaimat dough balls, sweet Ajwa dates, cardamom-warm qahwa coffee…
senses.feel: Hot desert sand under bare feet, the cool of a marble-floored mosque courtyard, smooth carved sandstone of a Nabataean tomb, the dry warmth of an Empty Quarter sunset, the soft heavy weight of a Saudi rug…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Who built the tombs of Hegra?
stories.body: Carved into sandstone cliffs in the AlUla valley are 111 enormous monumental tombs, each one cut directly into the rock with chisels. They were built by the Nabataean people about 2,000 years ago. The Nabataeans are best known for building Petra in modern Jordan, but Hegra (also called Mada'in Salih) was their second city, sitting on the spice and incense trade route between Yemen and the Mediterranean. After the Nabataeans were absorbed by Rome in 106 AD, the city was slowly abandoned. The carvings have been preserved beautifully by the dry desert. In 2008 Hegra became Saudi Arabia's very first UNESCO World Heritage site, and since 2019 visitors can finally tour it. Walking among the tombs at sunset, with sandstone cliffs glowing orange, feels like time travel.

stories.question: How big is the Empty Quarter?
stories.body: The Rub' al Khali ("Empty Quarter") is the LARGEST continuous sand desert on Earth: 650,000 square kilometres, bigger than France. It covers parts of Saudi Arabia, Oman, the UAE and Yemen. The dunes are some of the tallest in the world, up to 250 metres high. Almost nobody lives there. Daytime temperatures hit 55°C in summer; night temperatures drop close to freezing in winter. The Bedouin traditionally crossed it on camel caravans, knowing where every hidden well was, navigating by stars. The English explorer Wilfred Thesiger crossed it in the 1940s and wrote a famous book about it ("Arabian Sands"). Today you can do guided 4x4 trips into the edges of the Empty Quarter from Riyadh, sleeping in Bedouin tents under skies absolutely full of stars.

stories.question: What is Bedouin hospitality?
stories.body: Bedouin hospitality is one of the oldest unwritten social codes in Arabia. A stranger arriving at a tent (or these days, a house) is offered three small cups of cardamom coffee in a specific order, then dates, then food, then a place to sleep. By the unwritten law, a Bedouin family must protect any guest for three days and four nights, no questions asked, even if the guest is a stranger or even an enemy. The tradition lives on in modern Saudi homes. Visitors to Saudi Arabia are often surprised by how warmly they are welcomed, how much food is offered, and how much coffee is poured. The Arabic word "diwaniya" refers to the family room where guests are received, and Saudis often have nightly diwaniya gatherings of friends, coffee and conversation.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A culture has built welcoming strangers into its deepest traditions. What kind of welcome do you give a stranger at your own home?

stories.question: Why is Jeddah's old town special?
stories.body: Jeddah's historic district of Al-Balad is one of the most beautiful Arab old towns anywhere. Houses are made of coral-stone blocks quarried from the Red Sea, with intricate carved-wooden balconies (called "rawasheen") that overhang the narrow streets. For 1,400 years Jeddah was the main port for pilgrims sailing across the Red Sea to reach Mecca, and the city grew rich from welcoming them. Many of Al-Balad's buildings are 200 to 500 years old. UNESCO put Al-Balad on the World Heritage list in 2014. Walking through it at sunset, with the coral walls glowing orange and the call to prayer drifting from minarets, is one of the most atmospheric experiences in the Arab world.

stories.question: How is Saudi Arabia changing?
stories.body: In recent years, Saudi Arabia has been changing faster than almost any country on Earth. Tourist visas were introduced in 2019 (Saudi Arabia had been one of the most closed-off countries in the world before that). Women were allowed to drive starting in 2018. Public concerts, films, theme parks and music festivals were legalised after decades of bans. Mega-projects like AlUla's heritage developments and the futuristic NEOM city are under construction. Many traditions stay strong: religion is still central, the dress code is still modest, and Mecca and central Medina remain closed to non-Muslims. The country is in the middle of a real social and economic transformation. Visiting now means seeing both very old traditions and very new changes side by side.

convoQuestions.question: AlUla, Riyadh, Jeddah, desert, which has felt the most "Saudi Arabia"?
Did you sit through a Saudi coffee ritual? How was the rhythm of it?
How do the Hegra tombs compare to Petra in Jordan if you have seen both?
Saudi Arabia has been changing very fast. How does that feel as you visit it?

wordSearch.hint: Saudi foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Dromedary camel
animals.description: The single-hump Arabian camel is central to Saudi culture. Used historically for transport and for camel racing today.

animals.name: Arabian horse
animals.description: One of the oldest horse breeds on Earth, bred by Bedouin families for over 4,500 years. Famous for stamina and endurance.

animals.name: Nubian ibex
animals.description: A wild mountain goat with long curved horns, living on the cliffs of the Asir mountains and around AlUla.

animals.name: Saker falcon
animals.description: Trained for falconry, a traditional Saudi sport. Falconry is on the UNESCO heritage list.

animals.name: Yellow desert scorpion
animals.description: Active at night in the desert. Best admired with a torch and a respectful distance.

animals.name: Arabian oryx
animals.description: A beautiful pale-white antelope with long straight horns. Once extinct in the wild, reintroduced to Saudi desert reserves. Iconic and protected.

animals.name: Spinner dolphin
animals.description: Plays in pods in the Red Sea off Jeddah. The Saudi Red Sea has some of the cleanest, least-touched reefs anywhere.
